commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a standalone Codex safety-monitor bootstrap, write-capable MCP safety-stop tooling, a local SQLite audit outbox with optional Slack notifications, and a Codex app-server `denyRead` network-proxy config surface.

PR surface: Source +795, Tests +531, Docs +48, Other +5. Total +1379 across 19 files.

Reproducibility: not applicable. This PR adds new monitoring and safety-control capability rather than reporting a reproducible existing bug.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] A narrow code repair exists, but missing real behavior proof and the protected security-sensitive pilot surface make this a human-only merge blocker for now.

target.title: Add continuous safety monitoring for OpenClaw Codex sessions
target.body:
OpenClaw can keep Codex sessions active for long periods, but the existing Codex supervisor was primarily an operator-facing inspection surface. The internal pilot needs a separate monitor that stays alive, watches every target session through app-server APIs, and can stop one exact unsafe turn without wedging later work.

This adds the standalone monitor bootstrap and restricted MCP tools used by that process. A safety stop revalidates the active turn ID, interrupts it, cleans the thread's background terminals, waits for both the turn and processes to become inactive, and records the outcome in a local SQLite outbox.

The bootstrap uses Codex's existing persistent-mode lifecycle rather than maintaining a second continuation loop. It resumes or creates one monitor thread, enables that thread through the internal `thread/persistentMode/set` app-server API, and leaves persistence isolated to that thread. Deployments therefore require the matching pinned internal Codex build; there is no process-wide environment fallback. Headless bootstrap now carries a dedicated app-server capability token, so another process on the devbox cannot take over the monitor through its loopback socket.

The Codex app-server network profile also accepts explicit unreadable paths. OpenClaw folds those paths into the generated permissions profile, letting a deployment keep model credentials and supervisor runtime state visible to the host app server but masked from every sandboxed target tool.

The same outbox can optionally drain to one fixed Slack channel. Delivery remains outside the model, retries safely, uses the decision ID as Slack's client message ID, and persists the resulting channel and message timestamp. Alert content is bounded, redacted, and escaped so target transcript text cannot create Slack mentions.

The monitor is intentionally separate from the OpenClaw-controlled Codex process. Its write controls, target credential, policy prompt, audit database, and Slack token are only available to the trusted supervisor runtime.
target.author: pash-openai
